did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed HASCC, a hybrid algorithm for skin cancer classification using deep learning (VGG16 for feature extraction) and machine learning (XGBoost for classification) on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents HASCC, a novel hybrid algorithm for skin cancer classification, detailing its development using VGG16 and XGBoost on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes original experimental validation on a Raspberry Pi 4, comparing performance metrics (accuracy, execution time, resource usage) against other methods, which constitutes original research rather than a literature review.

title: HASCC: A Hybrid Algorithm for Skin Cancer Classification
brief_summary: This study introduces HASCC, a hybrid computer-aided diagnostic algorithm for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The method integrates image processing, feature extraction via VGG16 with PCA dimensionality reduction, and classification using XGBoost. The system was implemented on a Raspberry Pi 4 embedded device to evaluate both software performance and hardware resource efficiency. HASCC achieved accuracy scores ranging from 88.2% to 93.2% across three lesion classes with an average execution time of 250 milliseconds. Comparative analysis demonstrated that HASCC outperformed AutoKeras, LightGBM+GLCM, and PCA+VGG16 in terms of accuracy, response time, and resource consumption. The results confirm the viability of deploying this lightweight hybrid model on single-board microprocessors for efficient skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes used in the HASCC classification task on the HAM10000 dataset. In Table 1, accuracy for C1 was 93.2%.
One of the three classes used in the HASCC classification task on the HAM10000 dataset. In Table 1, accuracy for C2 was 88.5%.
One of the three classes used in the HASCC classification task on the HAM10000 dataset. In Table 1, accuracy for C3 was 88.2%.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer type (Carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper presents HASCC, a hybrid algorithm for skin cancer classification. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (C1: Carcinoma, C2: Benign keratosis, C3: Melanoma) using the HAM10000 dataset. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-Score for these three classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (ISIC Challenge skin cancer dataset)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of 10,015 pigmented dermoscopic image samples validated with different modalities. The images have a dimension of 600x450 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the XGBoost classifier after feature extraction via VGG16 and dimensionality reduction via PCA. The data distribution was split 60% for training and 40% for validation and testing.
use.validation: Used for validation as part of the 40% split (specific count not reported).
use.testing: Used for testing as part of the 40% split (specific count not reported).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance by counting true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class (C1, C2, C3). Used to derive precision, recall, and accuracy metrics.
To visualize the trade-off between sensitivity (true positive rate) and specificity (false positive rate) for the hybrid algorithm, allowing inference of sensitivity rates for each class.

Used for dimensionality reduction of features extracted by VGG16 (reducing components from 1,890 to 375) to mitigate class overlap and improve computational efficiency, rather than for statistical hypothesis testing.
To compare HASCC against AutoKeras, LightGBM+GLCM, and PCA+VGG16 using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-Score, response time, RAM usage, CPU usage, and power consumption.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-Score, execution time, resource usage) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 (ISIC-Archive).notes: HASCC achieved the highest accuracy for C1 and C2, and highest accuracy for C3 among the compared methods. HASCC had the shortest response time and lowest RAM usage. LightGBM+GLCM consumed the least power (2.95 W). HASCC training time was ~40x faster than AutoKeras, 1.39x faster than LightGBM+GLCM, and 1.76x faster than PCA+VGG16. HASCC response time was 7.6x faster than AutoKeras, 6x faster than LightGBM+GLCM, and 1.6x faster than PCA+VGG16.

Resnet101 (Ref [14]).Comparison: HASCC improved average classification performance by 5.75% compared to Resnet101.

InceptionV3 (Ref [14]).Comparison: HASCC improved average classification performance by 2.66% compared to InceptionV3.

Resnet50 (Ref [15]).Comparison: Resnet50 outperformed HASCC by 2.13% in accuracy. Note: This study used data augmentation on classes with small image counts.

MobileNet (Ref [16]).Comparison: HASCC improved performance by 9.83% over MobileNet.

Modified MobileNet (Ref [16]).Comparison: HASCC improved performance by 6.04% over Modified MobileNet. HASCC improved specificity by 5% over MobileNet and 1% over Modified MobileNet.

U-MobileNetV1 + U-DenseNet121 (Ref [17]).Comparison: Classification hits were 2.37% lower on average than HASCC.

Related Works Comparison.notes: Comparisons with related works were based on average accuracy or specific class accuracies where available. Ref [15] used all classes of HAM10000, while Ref [16] and [17] used specific subsets or architectures.

architecture_used.main_model: HASCC (Hybrid Algorithm for Skin Cancer Classification)
architecture_used.pretraining: VGG16 used for feature extraction; specific pretraining weights not explicitly detailed in context, but VGG16 is a standard pre-trained architecture.
architecture_used.input_size: Images from HAM10000 dataset (dimension 600x450 pixels); VGG16 input size mentioned in related work context as 227x227x3, but specific input size for HASCC's VGG16 stage is not explicitly stated.

architecture_details.dimensionality_reduction: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) reducing components from 1,890 to 375

training_details.hyperparameters: XGBoost used with default hyperparameters (mentioned in related work context for XGBoost, but not explicitly confirmed for HASCC implementation details in the provided text segments).

preprocessing_techniques: Sobel operator for edge detection (horizontal and vertical components)
Erosion morphology (cross-shaped structural element, kernel size 5x5) for body hair suppression
Binary thresholding (variable value 0-255 via GUI) for lesion region separation
Otsu adaptive thresholding (reinforcement threshold 127) for background lesion segmentation
Morphological aperture (elliptical structural element, k=5) to smooth contours
Superposition of median-smoothed image with thresholded region of interest
Resizing to 128x128 pixels in RGB scale for VGG16 input
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction of extracted features

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Not used in the HASCC algorithm. The paper notes that a related work (Ref [15], Resnet50) used data augmentation on classes with small image counts to increase proportions, but this was not applied to the proposed HASCC method.

conclusions: The proposed HASCC hybrid algorithm, which integrates VGG16 for feature extraction, PCA for dimensionality reduction, and XGBoost for classification, achieved accuracy scores ranging from 88.2% to 93.2% across three skin cancer classes (Carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Melanoma) on the HAM10000 dataset.

HASCC demonstrated superior software performance compared to AutoKeras, LightGBM+GLCM, and PCA+VGG16, achieving the highest accuracy for classes C1 and C2, and the highest accuracy for C3 among the compared methods.

HASCC exhibited the shortest response time (0.25 ms) and lowest RAM usage (42.7%) among the tested algorithms, making it significantly more efficient in terms of execution speed and resource consumption.
HASCC training time was approximately 40 times faster than AutoKeras, 1.39 times faster than LightGBM+GLCM, and 1.76 times faster than PCA+VGG16.

The hardware performance validation on a Raspberry Pi 4 embedded system confirms that HASCC is viable for implementation on single-board microprocessor devices due to its low power consumption (3.05 W) and efficient resource usage.
HASCC outperformed previous research works, improving average classification performance by 5.75% over Resnet101, 2.66% over InceptionV3, 9.83% over MobileNet, and 6.04% over Modified MobileNet.

While Resnet50 achieved a higher average accuracy (92.1%) than HASCC (85.66%), the authors note that this study utilized data augmentation on classes with small image counts, which may increase the risk of overfitting.
The reduction of PCA components from 1,890 to 375 contributed to improved execution time and reduced RAM and CPU usage, justifying the hybrid approach's efficiency.

The authors acknowledge that there is room for model optimization regarding feature extraction and classification parameters, specifically noting the susceptibility to over-fitting and over-learning if dataset data is not sufficiently improved.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the HAM10000 dataset without testing on independent clinical cohorts or diverse skin types.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons are based on descriptive metrics only.
The paper acknowledges a susceptibility to overfitting and over-learning, particularly noting that related work (Resnet50) used data augmentation which may artificially inflate accuracy.
The dataset class distribution is imbalanced, with the Carcinoma class (C1) showing the lowest accuracy (88.2%) and the authors note a need for more melanoma samples to improve differentiation.
Data augmentation was not applied to the proposed HASCC model, unlike some comparative works, which may limit its generalizability compared to models trained with augmented data.
The evaluation is limited to three specific lesion classes (Carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Melanoma), excluding other common HAM10000 classes such as dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, and nevus.
Hardware performance was tested only on a single Raspberry Pi 4 device, limiting the generalizability of the embedded system deployment claims to other hardware configurations.
The image preprocessing pipeline relies on manual segmentation thresholds via GUI sliders, which introduces potential user-dependent variability and lacks fully automated robustness.
